result.markdown:
--- title: "Let's Talk Regression Coefficients" subtitle: | Using Kaggle Student Education Data image: scale.jpg categories: [] date: 2026-03-15 draft: true # citation: # url: execute: message: false warning: false params: slug: regression-coefficient-interpretation date: 2025-08-25 --- ![Photo by [Piret Ilver](https://unsplash.com/@saltsup?utm_source=unsplash&utm_medium=referral&utm_content=creditCopyText) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/photos/brown-and-beige-weighing-scale-98MbUldcDJY?utm_source=unsplash&utm_medium=referral&utm_content=creditCopyText)](scale.jpg){fig-alt="A vintage scale with two slightly different trays for weighing objects." fig-align="left" width="6in" height="6in"} I told myself late last year that I would spend more time writing posts on topics I think are interesting. Similar to EVERY one of my new year resolutions, I didn't really stick with it. So after some thought, I wanted to focus on something that I am seeing often at UC Berkeley, a desire to focus strictly on *p* values rather than interest in regression coefficients. I am not sure why this is the case, but I think it may be due to different fields or coming at research projects through a machine learning lens. # Loading in the Data For this post, I will use Python, but the same can easily be accmoplished using R. Below I will be loading in the Python packages I will be using; however, the same could be accomplished with only `pandas`, `numpy`, and `statsmodels`. ::: {#loading-in-data .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import plotnine as pn import joblib from pyhere import here from janitor import clean_names pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None) pd.options.mode.copy_on_write = True df = pd.read_csv(here('posts/2026-03-11-interpretation/StudentPerformanceFactors.csv')).clean_names(case_type = 'snake') print(df.head()) print(df.columns.tolist()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` hours_studied attendance parental_involvement access_to_resources \ 0 23 84 Low High 1 19 64 Low Medium 2 24 98 Medium Medium 3 29 89 Low Medium 4 19 92 Medium Medium extracurricular_activities sleep_hours previous_scores motivation_level \ 0 No 7 73 Low 1 No 8 59 Low 2 Yes 7 91 Medium 3 Yes 8 98 Medium 4 Yes 6 65 Medium internet_access tutoring_sessions family_income teacher_quality \ 0 Yes 0 Low Medium 1 Yes 2 Medium Medium 2 Yes 2 Medium Medium 3 Yes 1 Medium Medium 4 Yes 3 Medium High school_type peer_influence physical_activity learning_disabilities \ 0 Public Positive 3 No 1 Public Negative 4 No 2 Public Neutral 4 No 3 Public Negative 4 No 4 Public Neutral 4 No parental_education_level distance_from_home gender exam_score 0 High School Near Male 67 1 College Moderate Female 61 2 Postgraduate Near Male 74 3 High School Moderate Male 71 4 College Near Female 70 ['hours_studied', 'attendance', 'parental_involvement', 'access_to_resources', 'extracurricular_activities', 'sleep_hours', 'previous_scores', 'motivation_level', 'internet_access', 'tutoring_sessions', 'family_income', 'teacher_quality', 'school_type', 'peer_influence', 'physical_activity', 'learning_disabilities', 'parental_education_level', 'distance_from_home', 'gender', 'exam_score'] ``` ::: ::: Since I am interested in inference rather than prediction, I am going to be focusing on individual predictors/features rather than looking at my model overall. The following predictors made the most conceptual sense to me as being associated with exam scores. ::: {#166cb39c .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # subset of data sub = df[['gender', 'exam_score', 'teacher_quality', 'previous_scores', 'sleep_hours', 'parental_involvement', 'attendance', 'hours_studied']] ``` ::: While there are better methods for creating dummy coded variables, for simplicity I got a quick look at distributions for the categorical variables to then split them into binary variables. I also dropped the original variables since they won't be needed for the models. I also decided to use the low categories for the `teacher_quality` and the `parental_involvement` as reference groups for my models. ::: {#7895d9b1 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} print(sub['gender'].value_counts(), '\n') print(sub['parental_involvement'].value_counts(), '\n') print(sub['teacher_quality'].value_counts(), '\n') # dummy coding variables sub['high_teach_qual'] = np.where(sub['teacher_quality'] == 'High', 1, 0) sub['med_teach_qual'] = np.where(sub['teacher_quality'] == 'Medium', 1, 0) sub['high_parent'] = np.where(sub['parental_involvement'] == 'High', 1, 0) sub['med_parent'] = np.where(sub['parental_involvement'] == 'Medium', 1, 0) sub['male'] = np.where(sub['gender'] == 'Male', 1, 0) sub = sub.drop(columns = ['gender', 'teacher_quality', 'parental_involvement']) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` gender Male 3814 Female 2793 Name: count, dtype: int64 parental_involvement Medium 3362 High 1908 Low 1337 Name: count, dtype: int64 teacher_quality Medium 3925 High 1947 Low 657 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: # Quick Visualizations I craeted some scatterplots to get a general understanding of the associations between students' previous scores, attendance, and the number of hours of sleep they are getting ::: {#213cceca .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} pn.ggplot.show( pn.ggplot(sub, pn.aes('previous_scores', 'exam_score')) + pn.geom_point(alpha = .25, color = 'seagreen') + pn.geom_smooth(method = 'lm', se = True) + pn.theme_light() ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-5-output-1.png){width=672 height=480} ::: ::: ::: {#209282f1 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} pn.ggplot.show( pn.ggplot(sub, pn.aes('attendance', 'exam_score')) + pn.geom_point(alpha = .25, color = 'seagreen') + pn.geom_smooth(method = 'lm', se = True) + pn.theme_light() ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-6-output-1.png){width=672 height=480} ::: ::: ::: {#71665aac .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} pn.ggplot.show( pn.ggplot(sub, pn.aes('hours_studied', 'exam_score')) + pn.geom_point(alpha = .25, color = 'seagreen') + pn.geom_smooth(method = 'lm', se = True) + pn.theme_light() ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-7-output-1.png){width=672 height=480} ::: ::: ::: {#8bf99c50 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} pn.ggplot.show( pn.ggplot(sub, pn.aes('sleep_hours', 'exam_score')) + pn.geom_point(alpha = .25, color = 'seagreen') + pn.geom_smooth(method = 'lm', se = True) + pn.theme_light() ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-8-output-1.png){width=672 height=480} ::: ::: We could see a slight positive association between previous exame scores and current exame scores. Interestingly, attendance and hours studied seem to have steeper positive associations, while hours of sleep appear to have no association with exam scores. We could look at how our dummy-coded predictors differ on exam scores to see if there is any differences between being in that group and not being in that group. If we look at the first dummy-coded variable (`high_parent`), we are comparing students that have high parental involvement and those without high parental involvement. ::: {#c62b5d2d .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} long = sub.melt(id_vars = ['exam_score', 'previous_scores', 'sleep_hours', 'attendance', 'hours_studied'], value_vars = ['high_teach_qual', 'med_teach_qual', 'high_parent', 'med_parent', 'male']) pn.ggplot.show( pn.ggplot(long, pn.aes('value', 'exam_score')) + pn.geom_boxplot(pn.aes(fill = 'factor(value)')) + pn.facet_wrap('variable') + pn.theme_light() + pn.theme(legend_position = 'none') ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-9-output-1.png){width=672 height=480} ::: ::: From a quick glance, there doesn't seem to be much difference for our dummy-coded variables. So we could now move on to linear regression models and the interpretations for the coefficients. # Modeling We will test ::: {#cd9fbfad .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} import statsmodels.api as sm def standard_coef(b, x, y): value = b * (x.std()/y.std()) return round(value, 2) def center_var(x): return x - x.mean() def z_transform(x): num = x - x.mean() dem = x.std() return round(num/dem, 2) sub[['zprevious_scores', 'zsleep_hours', 'zattendance', 'zhours_studied']] = z_transform(sub[['previous_scores', 'sleep_hours', 'attendance', 'hours_studied']]) sub[['cprevious_scores', 'csleep_hours', 'cattendance', 'chours_studied']] = center_var(sub[['previous_scores', 'sleep_hours', 'attendance', 'hours_studied']]) ``` ::: ## Simple Regression ::: {#9984dd9c .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} y = sub['exam_score'] x = sub['previous_scores'] x = sm.add_constant(x) simple_model = sm.OLS(y, x) simple_fit = simple_model.fit() simple_fit.summary() standard_coef(b = simple_fit.params[1], x = sub['previous_scores'], y = sub['exam_score']) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=10} ``` np.float64(0.18) ``` ::: ::: ::: {#303c5c89 .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} xc = sub['cprevious_scores'] xc = sm.add_constant(xc) simplec_model = sm.OLS(y, xc) simplec_fit = simplec_model.fit() simplec_fit.summary() standard_coef(b = simplec_fit.params[1], x = sub['cprevious_scores'], y = sub['exam_score']) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=11} ``` np.float64(0.18) ``` ::: ::: ::: {#8adacc3f .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} xz = sub['zprevious_scores'] xz = sm.add_constant(xz) simplez_model = sm.OLS(y, xz) simplez_fit = simplez_model.fit() simplez_fit.summary() standard_coef(b = simplez_fit.params[1], x = sub['zprevious_scores'], y = sub['exam_score']) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ``` np.float64(0.18) ``` ::: ::: ## Multiple Regression ::: {#cceb7427 .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} x_main = sub.loc[:, 'previous_scores':'med_parent'] x_main = sm.add_constant(x_main) main_model = sm.OLS(y, x_main) main_fit = main_model.fit() main_fit.summary() main_beta = [] for i in np.arange(0, x_main.shape[1], 1): beta_values = standard_coef(b = main_fit.params[i], x = x_main.iloc[:, i], y = y) main_beta.append(beta_values) main_param = main_fit.params.reset_index() main_fit_param = pd.DataFrame({'b': main_param.iloc[:, 1], 'beta': pd.Series(main_beta)}).round(2) main_fit_param['coef'] = main_param.iloc[:, 0] main_fit_param = main_fit_param[['coef', 'b', 'beta']] main_fit_param ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=13} ```{=html}
coef b beta
0 const 40.47 0.00
1 previous_scores 0.05 0.18
2 sleep_hours -0.02 -0.01
3 attendance 0.20 0.59
4 hours_studied 0.29 0.45
5 high_teach_qual 0.94 0.11
6 med_teach_qual 0.40 0.05
7 high_parent 1.92 0.22
8 med_parent 0.90 0.12
``` ::: ::: ::: {#8f61658a .cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} z_main = sub[sub.filter(regex = r'^z').columns.tolist() + sub[['male', 'high_teach_qual', 'med_teach_qual', 'high_parent', 'med_parent']].columns.tolist()] z_main = sm.add_constant(z_main) mainz_model = sm.OLS(y, z_main) mainz_fit = mainz_model.fit() mainz_fit.summary() mainz_beta = [] for i in np.arange(0, z_main.shape[1], 1): beta_values = standard_coef(b = mainz_fit.params[i], x = z_main.iloc[:, i], y = y) mainz_beta.append(beta_values) mainz_param = mainz_fit.params.reset_index() mainz_fit_param = pd.DataFrame({'b': mainz_param.iloc[:, 1], 'beta': pd.Series(mainz_beta)}).round(2) mainz_fit_param['coef'] = mainz_param['index'] mainz_fit_param = mainz_fit_param[['coef', 'b', 'beta']] mainz_fit_param ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=14} ```{=html}
coef b beta
0 const 65.72 0.00
1 zprevious_scores 0.70 0.18
2 zsleep_hours -0.03 -0.01
3 zattendance 2.30 0.59
4 zhours_studied 1.75 0.45
5 male -0.02 -0.00
6 high_teach_qual 0.94 0.11
7 med_teach_qual 0.40 0.05
8 high_parent 1.92 0.22
9 med_parent 0.90 0.12
``` ::: ::: ## Interaction Model ::: {#7a4d736c .cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} sub['study_x_attend'] = sub['hours_studied'] * sub['attendance'] print(sub[['hours_studied', 'attendance', 'study_x_attend']].corr()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` hours_studied attendance study_x_attend hours_studied 1.000000 -0.009908 0.893230 attendance -0.009908 1.000000 0.421173 study_x_attend 0.893230 0.421173 1.000000 ``` ::: ::: Our correlation matrix shows that there is a .89 correlation between our interaction and hours studied. This is problematic for linear models due to multicollinearity. This is where centering our predictors becomes crucial because my centering our predictors we remove this issue of multicollinearity. ::: {#60bc53f9 .cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} c_int = sub[sub.filter(regex = r'^c').columns.tolist() + sub[['male', 'high_teach_qual', 'med_teach_qual', 'high_parent', 'med_parent']].columns.tolist()] c_int['study_x_attend'] = c_int['chours_studied'] * c_int['cattendance'] print(c_int.head()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` cprevious_scores csleep_hours cattendance chours_studied male \ 0 -2.070531 -0.02906 4.022552 3.024671 1 1 -16.070531 0.97094 -15.977448 -0.975329 0 2 15.929469 -0.02906 18.022552 4.024671 1 3 22.929469 0.97094 9.022552 9.024671 1 4 -10.070531 -1.02906 12.022552 -0.975329 0 high_teach_qual med_teach_qual high_parent med_parent study_x_attend 0 0 1 0 0 12.166895 1 0 1 0 0 15.583272 2 0 1 0 1 72.534838 3 0 1 0 0 81.425560 4 1 0 0 1 -11.725946 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#f33c5980 .cell execution_count=17} ``` {.python .cell-code} c_int = sm.add_constant(c_int) c_int_model = sm.OLS(y, c_int) c_int_fit = c_int_model.fit() print(c_int_fit.summary()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` OLS Regression Results ============================================================================== Dep. Variable: exam_score R-squared: 0.608 Model: OLS Adj. R-squared: 0.608 Method: Least Squares F-statistic: 1025. Date: Sat, 21 Mar 2026 Prob (F-statistic): 0.00 Time: 00:13:31 Log-Likelihood: -15252. No. Observations: 6607 AIC: 3.053e+04 Df Residuals: 6596 BIC: 3.060e+04 Df Model: 10 Covariance Type: nonrobust ==================================================================================== coef std err t P>|t| [0.025 0.975] ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ const 65.7214 0.113 583.245 0.000 65.501 65.942 cprevious_scores 0.0484 0.002 23.214 0.000 0.044 0.052 csleep_hours -0.0206 0.020 -1.007 0.314 -0.061 0.019 cattendance 0.1989 0.003 76.596 0.000 0.194 0.204 chours_studied 0.2922 0.005 58.368 0.000 0.282 0.302 male -0.0174 0.061 -0.287 0.774 -0.136 0.102 high_teach_qual 0.9371 0.106 8.882 0.000 0.730 1.144 med_teach_qual 0.4004 0.098 4.087 0.000 0.208 0.592 high_parent 1.9207 0.087 22.089 0.000 1.750 2.091 med_parent 0.8962 0.079 11.373 0.000 0.742 1.051 study_x_attend 0.0006 0.000 1.438 0.151 -0.000 0.001 ============================================================================== Omnibus: 8230.375 Durbin-Watson: 2.010 Prob(Omnibus): 0.000 Jarque-Bera (JB): 1360381.884 Skew: 6.775 Prob(JB): 0.00 Kurtosis: 71.978 Cond. No. 392. ============================================================================== Notes: [1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified. ``` ::: ::: ::: {#7416cc57 .cell execution_count=18} ``` {.python .cell-code} c_int_beta = [] for i in np.arange(0, c_int.shape[1], 1): beta_values = standard_coef(b = c_int_fit.params[i], x = c_int.iloc[:, i], y = y) c_int_beta.append(beta_values) c_int_param = c_int_fit.params.reset_index() c_int_fit_param = pd.DataFrame({'b': c_int_param.iloc[:, 1], 'beta': pd.Series(c_int_beta)}).round(2) c_int_fit_param['coef'] = c_int_param['index'] c_int_fit_param = c_int_fit_param[['coef', 'b', 'beta']] print(c_int_fit_param) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` coef b beta 0 const 65.72 0.00 1 cprevious_scores 0.05 0.18 2 csleep_hours -0.02 -0.01 3 cattendance 0.20 0.59 4 chours_studied 0.29 0.45 5 male -0.02 -0.00 6 high_teach_qual 0.94 0.11 7 med_teach_qual 0.40 0.05 8 high_parent 1.92 0.22 9 med_parent 0.90 0.12 10 study_x_attend 0.00 0.01 ``` ::: :::
